title: Create a new Next.js application with create-next-app
summary: Scaffold a new Next.js project using create-next-app with App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and recommended defaults.
declaration: I will create a new Next.js project using `npx create-next-app@latest` with App Router, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS enabled. The project will use the `src/` directory and default import alias `@/*`. If you want Pages Router, JavaScript, or a different configuration, say so before I begin.
instructions:
## Create a New Next.js Application ### 1. Pre-flight checks - Verify Node.js 18.18+ is installed: run `node -v`. If below 18.18, stop and upgrade first. - Verify npm or another package manager is available: `npm -v` (or `pnpm -v`, `yarn -v`, `bun -v`). - Confirm the target directory does not already contain a `package.json` — do NOT scaffold inside an existing project unless the user explicitly wants a monorepo sub-app. - If a directory with the desired project name already exists, warn the user before proceeding. ### 2. Run create-next-app - Run the scaffolding command with recommended defaults: ```bash npx create-next-app@latest \ --typescript \ --tailwind \ --eslint \ --app \ --src-dir \ --import-alias "@/*" \ --use-npm ``` - Flag breakdown: - `--typescript`: Enables TypeScript (creates `.ts`/`.tsx` files, includes `tsconfig.json`). - `--tailwind`: Installs and configures Tailwind CSS. - `--eslint`: Sets up ESLint with Next.js recommended rules. - `--app`: Uses the App Router (`src/app/` directory) instead of Pages Router. - `--src-dir`: Places source code inside `src/` for cleaner project root. - `--import-alias "@/*"`: Configures `@/` as the import alias for `src/`. - `--use-npm`: Uses npm as the package manager. Replace with `--use-pnpm`, `--use-yarn`, or `--use-bun` based on user preference. - If the user has not specified a project name, ask for one. Do NOT use generic names like `my-app`. - Wait for the scaffolding to complete. It installs dependencies automatically. ### 3. Verify the scaffold succeeded - `cd` into the project directory. - Verify these files/directories exist: - `package.json` with `next`, `react`, `react-dom` in dependencies. - `tsconfig.json` with `paths: { "@/*": ["./src/*"] }`. - `next.config.ts` (or `next.config.mjs`). - `src/app/layout.tsx` — the root layout. - `src/app/page.tsx` — the home page. - `src/app/globals.css` — Tailwind CSS entry point. - `tailwind.config.ts` (v3) or `postcss.config.mjs` (v4, depending on create-next-app version). - `.eslintrc.json` (or `eslint.config.mjs` for flat config). - Verify `node_modules/` exists (dependencies were installed). - Check the installed Next.js version: `npx next --version`. ### 4. Start the development server - Run `npm run dev`. - Open `http://localhost:3000` in a browser. - Confirm the default Next.js welcome page loads without errors. - Check the terminal for any warnings or errors. ### 5. Clean up the default template - The scaffold includes demo content that should be replaced. Remove or simplify: - `src/app/page.tsx` — replace the entire default content with a minimal component: ```tsx export default function Home() { return (

Hello

); } ``` - `src/app/globals.css` — keep only the Tailwind import and CSS variable definitions. Remove the demo gradient styles. - Delete `public/next.svg` and `public/vercel.svg` if not needed. - Do NOT delete `src/app/layout.tsx`, `src/app/globals.css`, `next.config.ts`, or `tsconfig.json`. ### 6. Verify tsconfig.json paths - Open `tsconfig.json` and confirm `paths` is set: ```json { "compilerOptions": { "paths": { "@/*": ["./src/*"] } } } ``` - If `src/` directory is not used, paths should be `"@/*": ["./*"]`. - This alias is critical — most skills and libraries (shadcn/ui, Vitest, etc.) depend on `@/` resolving correctly. ### 7. Configure .gitignore - Verify `.gitignore` includes at minimum: - `node_modules/` - `.next/` - `.env*.local` - `*.tsbuildinfo` - `next-env.d.ts` - Add `.env` and `.env.local` if not already listed. - Add `coverage/` if tests will be added later. ### 8. Initialize git (if not already) - If the directory is not a git repo: `git init`. - Create an initial commit: `git add -A && git commit -m "init: scaffold Next.js project"`. - Do NOT push to a remote unless the user asks. ### 9. Optional: configure VS Code settings - If the project uses VS Code, create `.vscode/settings.json` with: ```json { "typescript.tsdk": "node_modules/typescript/lib", "editor.defaultFormatter": "esbenp.prettier-vscode", "editor.formatOnSave": true, "editor.codeActionsOnSave": { "source.fixAll.eslint": "explicit" } } ``` - Only create this if the user uses VS Code or does not specify an editor. ### 10. Project structure overview After cleanup, the project should look like: ``` / src/ app/ layout.tsx # Root layout (html, body, fonts, metadata) page.tsx # Home page globals.css # Tailwind imports + CSS variables public/ # Static assets next.config.ts # Next.js configuration tsconfig.json # TypeScript configuration with @/ alias tailwind.config.ts # Tailwind configuration (if v3) postcss.config.mjs # PostCSS configuration package.json .eslintrc.json # ESLint rules .gitignore ``` ### Common Errors & Fixes - **`npm ERR! code ENOENT` or `command not found: npx`** -- Node.js is not installed or not in PATH. Install Node.js 18.18+ from nodejs.org or via nvm: `nvm install 18`. - **`EACCES permission denied`** -- npm global directory has permission issues. Fix with `sudo chown -R $(whoami) $(npm config get prefix)/{lib/node_modules,bin,share}` or use nvm which avoids sudo entirely. - **Port 3000 already in use** -- Another process is using port 3000. Either kill it (`lsof -ti:3000 | xargs kill`) or start Next.js on a different port: `npm run dev -- -p 3001`. - **`Module not found: Can't resolve '@/...'`** -- The `@/*` path alias is misconfigured. Verify `tsconfig.json` has `paths: { "@/*": ["./src/*"] }` and the `src/` directory exists. If not using `src/`, set `"@/*": ["./*"]`. - **Tailwind classes not applying** -- Check that `globals.css` is imported in `layout.tsx` and contains the Tailwind import (`@import "tailwindcss"` for v4, or `@tailwind base; @tailwind components; @tailwind utilities;` for v3). Also verify `postcss.config.mjs` has the correct plugin. - **ESLint errors on fresh project** -- Run `npx next lint` to see errors. If rules are too strict, edit `.eslintrc.json` to extend only `next/core-web-vitals`. For flat config, check `eslint.config.mjs`. - **TypeScript `Cannot find module 'next/...'` in IDE** -- Run `npm install` to ensure all types are installed. Restart the TypeScript server in your editor (VS Code: Cmd+Shift+P > TypeScript: Restart TS Server). - **`next.config.mjs` vs `next.config.ts`** -- Next.js 15+ supports `.ts` config natively. Older versions use `.mjs`. Do not mix formats. Use whichever create-next-app generated. ### What NOT to Do - Do NOT run `create-next-app` inside an existing project directory with a `package.json` -- it will fail or create a nested mess. Always scaffold in a new directory. - Do NOT use `--no-eslint` to skip ESLint setup -- ESLint catches real bugs in Next.js (missing keys, invalid image usage, etc.). Keep it. - Do NOT change the import alias from `@/*` to something non-standard unless the user explicitly requests it -- nearly all Next.js ecosystem tools and tutorials assume `@/*`. - Do NOT delete `layout.tsx` -- it is required by the App Router. Every route segment needs a layout, and the root layout must include `` and `` tags. - Do NOT use Pages Router (`--no-app`) for new projects unless the user specifically needs it -- App Router is the default and recommended architecture since Next.js 13.4. - Do NOT skip `--typescript` for new projects -- TypeScript catches entire categories of bugs at build time. The performance cost is zero at runtime. - Do NOT manually install `next`, `react`, `react-dom` separately -- `create-next-app` handles dependency installation with correct version compatibility. - Do NOT commit `node_modules/` or `.next/` to git -- they are generated artifacts and must be in `.gitignore`. - Do NOT install global packages (`npm install -g next`) -- use `npx` for CLI tools to always get the latest version.
